title: Claude Is Not a Compiler
excerpt: In early 2025, I wrote Is Claude a Compiler? At the time, my answer was: I don’t know.
content_html:
In early 2025, I wrote Is Claude a Compiler? At the time, my answer was: I don’t know.

I’m now pretty sure the answer is “no, that’s a category error, it’s better than a compiler.” But this requires a bit of unpacking.

Computer programs are notoriously intricate and finicky. A program operates at an extreme level of precision. There is no “wave hands” CPU instruction. High-level goals, meanwhile, are deeply underspecified.

In a highly stylized view of the world, software gets built in layers, each one adding specification and hiding “unnecessary” detail. Vision becomes strategy, product plans become coding plans, code becomes binaries. Each step is handled by a different role: executive, VP, PM, architect, engineer, compiler. 

Critically, every step involves making lots of decisions. That’s what it means to increase the level of specification. (This is why one of my two key metrics for hiring engineers is judgment. The other is comity.)

The bottom layer, from source code to binary, is what a compiler does. Compilers make lots of decisions! Inlining, register allocation, whether to emit warnings or reject a program outright. And these decisions matter: They drive performance, system stability, predictability, and failure modes. A compiler engineer’s job is to arrange for the compiler to make consistently good decisions.

A good, trusted compiler frees a software engineer from having to make these decisions. Most engineers have little idea how compilers work; they don’t need to in order to be effective. 

In 2025, we operated in a world where we used LLMs to generate smallish chunks of code. In this mental model, a coding agent might slot in as a new layer between a software engineer and a traditional compiler. It “compiles” natural language to code, making decisions so the engineer doesn’t have to. Its value is proportional to its reliability and the scale of the decisions it can make.

The thing is, this highly stylized view of the world is false. Abstractions leak and layers rub. And even if they didn’t, we’d poke holes in them anyway.

Working across layers is extremely valuable; mechanical sympathy matters.

Part of how the Empire State Building was constructed in under a year and under budget (!!) was by systematically working across layers. For example, when deciding about the exterior chrome-nickel steel cladding:

Neither architects, builders nor subcontractors felt competent to deal with this complicated technical problem of construction without full consultation. Accordingly, after full preliminary discussion, an all-inclusive meeting was called which was attended by representatives of the owner, the architects and builders, the subcontractors rolling the material, the metal workers who were to fabricate and those who were to erect it, and the inspectors who were to test all sheets at the several stages of preparation.

This sounds really obvious when you say it out loud.

And yet we systematically fail at this in practice. I can only imagine the delight of the metal workers who had an opportunity to guide the design toward something that wasn’t slow and miserable to work on.

Part of the reason we fail is ignorance of what is even worth asking about. There’s a reason that the best executives have deep knowledge of their industry. I also suspect that some of it is dismissiveness (“What could a line metalworker have to tell me?”). But a big chunk is also communication and organizational overhead. Layers exist for a reason—information hiding enables organizational scaling.

Claude is better than a compiler because it can work vertically across the stack. LLMs now talk strategy, product, architecture, code, and machine code. It can’t (yet?) do most individual tasks as well as an experienced, dedicated human, but it can do all of them, without having to schedule meetings or ask permission.

Here’s a concrete example.

exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz. When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three. Easy, right?

But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.

We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth. And life was good.

But latency matters, so we added regions. And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon. Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages. To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.

We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat. We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.

The goals were clear: Reduce latency for users far from Oregon and increase uptime resiliency. But the rest was not. We had to figure out everything from the exact behavior we wanted (particularly under various failure conditions), to how it fit into our overall company plans, to the architecture that could best achieve those goals, straight through down to the fine implementation details.

We hashed out the highest level strategic and architectural decisions in person. We’d make a fairly general-purpose DNS server and layer on our particular behavioral tweaks, use a hub-and-spoke model, use an append-only replication strategy, and have persistence at the edges.

All that was left was to actually build it.

I had LLMs research standard designs for distributed DNS systems, teach me about the guts and quirks of DNS, point out historic security failings, explore alternative implementation strategies (AXFR/IXFR? no thanks), research open source offerings, game out failure modes, and plan testing strategies.

Once I had an initial sketch of a design that seemed promising, I prompted multiple concurrent agent loops into building the entire thing, including tests and adversarial code review. They raised a bunch of questions—at every level of detail, from major structural approaches down to line-level code concerns. As I answered them (or reverted answers that generated regret), I slowly converted what I had learned into very terse written guidance, codifying decisions that proved to be important.

Then I asked new agents to compare the completed implementations and look for interesting deviations. It was shocking how many important decisions the agents never asked about but simply made—and made differently.

Here’s an example. Replication uses the fairly obvious approach: Catch up by asking for everything since the last known entry, and then long poll for new entries. There’s one ugly twist: database rollbacks. Rare, but they do happen, and they break the “append-only” contract.

The agents noticed this, and they solved it in wildly different ways. The design I ultimately settled on was to give every row a “timeline” field, as in “which timeline are you living in?” These are randomly generated, and every sync request for “entries since row N” includes the edge server’s timeline value for row N. If there’s a timeline mismatch, we know that history has been altered and fall back to a full clean re-sync.

There were also obvious style differences between the systems built by different agents. Claude and Codex both agreed that Claude created a more elegant system but that Codex was more thorough.

I worked through the list of major identified divergences, experimented, and then added more written guidance.

Then I repeated that entire differential spec analysis process, twice. I know my aphorisms.

Plan to throw one away; you will, anyhow.

— Fred Brooks

If you plan to throw one away, you will throw away two.

— Craig Zerouni

By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.

The final system included unit tests, end-to-end tests, a shadow-mode for de-risking prod rollout, and a terse written-by-and-for-agents doc suite.

This cumulatively took about a week of my attention. I read a vanishingly small amount of the actual code.

At the end of that, I presented the solution to the team. I planned to launch the server and then go on vacation. As my colleagues peppered me with questions—"How does X work? What happens in condition Y?"—I found I could answer all of them confidently. (And I did go on that vacation. Number of DNS incidents a month later: 0.)

Claude wasn’t just a compiler here. I never handed off a task and let an agent make a bunch of decisions in order to reduce it to practice. That’s vibe-coding.

Rather, Claude was a vertically integrated resource, a multi-compiler. Its ability to work across the stack accelerated and augmented my ability to make a bunch of decisions at different levels, including about which decisions were important. (Most individual lines of code don’t make that cut.) That’s vibe-engineering.

I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code. Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve. But I won't have to. And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work. And there’s an enduring artifact that encapsulates the central, intentional aspects of the design that were important enough to record, across all layers, and should thus survive bug fixes and code churn.

One of the questions of this era is: What do software engineers need to understand about the systems they work on?

Well-chosen layers provide understanding. Fundamental laws of physics appear all-encompassing, but they’re inferior to classical mechanics for explaining why it’s better to be in a bus than a car in an accident.

Some software layers are dying, because they provide convenience, but not extra insight. (Sorry, Tailwind. I loved you.) But software layers that enable us to express important decisions in a comprehensible way? Those will stay.

We are shifting more of our attention up the stack, but without fully relinquishing the lower layers. Agents are not a free pass to hand off all understanding of the deeper layers of a system. Most of the Go standard library is written in Go, but a few key routines are written in assembly. You can’t rely on the compiler there.

Software engineers are being stretched. It’s exhilarating and exhausting. What’s becoming clear, though, is that in the near future, vibe-engineering is just…engineering.

sentences.zh: 2025 年初，我写了《克劳德是编译器吗？》

sentences.en: At the time, my answer was: I don’t know.
sentences.zh: 当时我的回答是：我不知道。

sentences.en: But this requires a bit of unpacking.
sentences.zh: 但这需要一些拆包。

sentences.en: High-level goals, meanwhile, are deeply underspecified.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，高层目标却非常不明确。

sentences.en: Accordingly, after full preliminary discussion, an all-inclusive meeting was called which was attended by representatives of the owner, the architects and builders, the subcontractors rolling the material, the metal workers who were to fabricate and those who were to erect it, and the inspectors who were to test all sheets at the several stages of preparation.
sentences.zh: 因此，经过充分的初步讨论后，召开了一次全体会议，参加会议的有业主代表、建筑师和建造商、轧制材料的分包商、制造和安装金属工人以及在准备的几个阶段对所有板材进行测试的检查员。

blocks.html_en: This sounds really obvious when you say it out loud.

sentences.en: This sounds really obvious when you say it out loud.
sentences.zh: 当你大声说出来时，这听起来真的很明显。

blocks.html_en: And yet we systematically fail at this in practice. I can only imagine the delight of the metal workers who had an opportunity to guide the design toward something that wasn’t slow and miserable to work on.

sentences.en: And yet we systematically fail at this in practice.
sentences.zh: 然而，我们在实践中却系统性地失败了。

sentences.en: I can only imagine the delight of the metal workers who had an opportunity to guide the design toward something that wasn’t slow and miserable to work on.
sentences.zh: 我只能想象那些金属工人有机会引导设计走向不那么缓慢和痛苦的工作的喜悦。

blocks.html_en: Part of the reason we fail is ignorance of what is even worth asking about. There’s a reason that the best executives have deep knowledge of their industry. I also suspect that some of it is dismissiveness (“What could a line metalworker have to tell me?”). But a big chunk is also communication and organizational overhead. Layers exist for a reason—information hiding enables organizational scaling.

sentences.en: Part of the reason we fail is ignorance of what is even worth asking about.
sentences.zh: 我们失败的部分原因是不知道什么是值得问的。

sentences.en: There’s a reason that the best executives have deep knowledge of their industry.
sentences.zh: 最优秀的高管对其行业有着深入的了解，这是有原因的。

sentences.en: I also suspect that some of it is dismissiveness (“What could a line metalworker have to tell me?”).
sentences.zh: 我还怀疑其中一些是不屑一顾（“生产线金属工人能告诉我什么？”）。

sentences.en: But a big chunk is also communication and organizational overhead.
sentences.zh: 但很大一部分还在于沟通和组织开销。

sentences.en: Layers exist for a reason—information hiding enables organizational scaling.
sentences.zh: 层的存在是有原因的——信息隐藏可以实现组织的扩展。

blocks.html_en: Claude is better than a compiler because it can work vertically across the stack. LLMs now talk strategy, product, architecture, code, and machine code. It can’t (yet?) do most individual tasks as well as an experienced, dedicated human, but it can do all of them, without having to schedule meetings or ask permission.

sentences.en: Claude is better than a compiler because it can work vertically across the stack.
sentences.zh: Claude 比编译器更好，因为它可以跨堆栈垂直工作。

sentences.en: LLMs now talk strategy, product, architecture, code, and machine code.
sentences.zh: 法学硕士现在讨论战略、产品、架构、代码和机器代码。

sentences.en: It can’t (yet?)
sentences.zh: 还不能（还？）

sentences.en: do most individual tasks as well as an experienced, dedicated human, but it can do all of them, without having to schedule meetings or ask permission.
sentences.zh: 它可以像经验丰富、专注的人一样完成大多数个人任务，但它可以完成所有这些任务，而无需安排会议或征求许可。

blocks.html_en: Here’s a concrete example.

sentences.en: Here’s a concrete example.
sentences.zh: 这是一个具体的例子。

blocks.html_en: exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz. When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three. Easy, right?

sentences.en: exe.dev VMs have nice domain names: vm-name.exe.xyz.
sentences.zh: exe.dev VM 有一个很好听的域名：vm-name.exe.xyz。

sentences.en: When we start a new VM, we add a CNAME entry or three.
sentences.zh: 当我们启动新的虚拟机时，我们会添加一个或三个 CNAME 条目。

sentences.en: Easy, right?
sentences.zh: 容易，对吧？

blocks.html_en: But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.

sentences.en: But our VMs start fast, so fast that even if we created the DNS entries before creating the VM, our users still had to sit around waiting for DNS to propagate, which occasionally took minutes, not seconds.
sentences.zh: 但我们的虚拟机启动速度非常快，即使我们在创建虚拟机之前创建了 DNS 条目，我们的用户仍然必须等待 DNS 传播，这有时需要几分钟而不是几秒钟。

blocks.html_en: We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth. And life was good.

sentences.en: We did the obvious thing: We wrote our own DNS server, so that DNS always immediately matched the source of truth.
sentences.zh: 我们做了一件显而易见的事情：我们编写了自己的 DNS 服务器，以便 DNS 始终立即匹配事实来源。

sentences.en: And life was good.
sentences.zh: 生活是美好的。

blocks.html_en: But latency matters, so we added regions. And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon. Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages. To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.

sentences.en: But latency matters, so we added regions.
sentences.zh: 但延迟很重要，因此我们添加了区域。

sentences.en: And just like that, DNS became the long pole again, because all DNS was served out of Oregon.
sentences.zh: 就这样，DNS 再次成为长杆，因为所有 DNS 均由俄勒冈州提供服务。

sentences.en: Also, deployments caused tiny DNS outages.
sentences.zh: 此外，部署还导致了轻微的 DNS 中断。

sentences.en: To fix this, all we needed now was a geographically distributed but fully consistent DNS server.
sentences.zh: 为了解决这个问题，我们现在需要的是一个地理上分散但完全一致的 DNS 服务器。

blocks.html_en: We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat. We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.

sentences.en: We did what a sensible engineer does when faced with a hard problem: cheat.
sentences.zh: 我们做了一个明智的工程师在面对难题时会做的事情：作弊。

sentences.en: We vibe-engineered a distributed DNS server tuned to our specific needs.
sentences.zh: 我们根据我们的特定需求设计了一个分布式 DNS 服务器。

blocks.html_en: The goals were clear: Reduce latency for users far from Oregon and increase uptime resiliency. But the rest was not. We had to figure out everything from the exact behavior we wanted (particularly under various failure conditions), to how it fit into our overall company plans, to the architecture that could best achieve those goals, straight through down to the fine implementation details.

sentences.en: The goals were clear: Reduce latency for users far from Oregon and increase uptime resiliency.
sentences.zh: 目标很明确：减少远离俄勒冈州的用户的延迟并提高正常运行时间的弹性。

sentences.en: But the rest was not.
sentences.zh: 但其余的则不然。

sentences.en: We had to figure out everything from the exact behavior we wanted (particularly under various failure conditions), to how it fit into our overall company plans, to the architecture that could best achieve those goals, straight through down to the fine implementation details.
sentences.zh: 我们必须弄清楚一切，从我们想要的确切行为（特别是在各种故障条件下），到它如何适应我们的整体公司计划，到最能实现这些目标的架构，一直到精细的实施细节。

blocks.html_en: We hashed out the highest level strategic and architectural decisions in person. We’d make a fairly general-purpose DNS server and layer on our particular behavioral tweaks, use a hub-and-spoke model, use an append-only replication strategy, and have persistence at the edges.

sentences.en: We hashed out the highest level strategic and architectural decisions in person.
sentences.zh: 我们亲自讨论了最高级别的战略和架构决策。

sentences.en: We’d make a fairly general-purpose DNS server and layer on our particular behavioral tweaks, use a hub-and-spoke model, use an append-only replication strategy, and have persistence at the edges.
sentences.zh: 我们将制作一个相当通用的 DNS 服务器，并根据我们的特定行为调整进行分层，使用中心辐射模型，使用仅附加复制策略，并在边缘具有持久性。

blocks.html_en: All that was left was to actually build it.

sentences.en: All that was left was to actually build it.
sentences.zh: 剩下的就是实际构建它。

blocks.html_en: I had LLMs research standard designs for distributed DNS systems, teach me about the guts and quirks of DNS, point out historic security failings, explore alternative implementation strategies (AXFR/IXFR? no thanks), research open source offerings, game out failure modes, and plan testing strategies.

sentences.en: I had LLMs research standard designs for distributed DNS systems, teach me about the guts and quirks of DNS, point out historic security failings, explore alternative implementation strategies (AXFR/IXFR?
sentences.zh: 我让法学硕士研究分布式 DNS 系统的标准设计，教我 DNS 的本质和怪癖，指出历史上的安全缺陷，探索替代实施策略（AXFR/IXFR？

sentences.en: no thanks), research open source offerings, game out failure modes, and plan testing strategies.
sentences.zh: 不，谢谢），研究开源产品、游戏失败模式并计划测试策略。

blocks.html_en: Once I had an initial sketch of a design that seemed promising, I prompted multiple concurrent agent loops into building the entire thing, including tests and adversarial code review. They raised a bunch of questions—at every level of detail, from major structural approaches down to line-level code concerns. As I answered them (or reverted answers that generated regret), I slowly converted what I had learned into very terse written guidance, codifying decisions that proved to be important.

sentences.en: Once I had an initial sketch of a design that seemed promising, I prompted multiple concurrent agent loops into building the entire thing, including tests and adversarial code review.
sentences.zh: 一旦我有了一个看起来有希望的设计的初步草图，我就提示多个并发代理循环来构建整个东西，包括测试和对抗性代码审查。

sentences.en: They raised a bunch of questions—at every level of detail, from major structural approaches down to line-level code concerns.
sentences.zh: 他们提出了一系列问题——从主要结构方法到行级代码问题，涉及各个细节级别。

sentences.en: As I answered them (or reverted answers that generated regret), I slowly converted what I had learned into very terse written guidance, codifying decisions that proved to be important.
sentences.zh: 当我回答这些问题（或者恢复那些引起遗憾的答案）时，我慢慢地将我所学到的知识转化为非常简洁的书面指导，将事实证明很重要的决定编入法典。

blocks.html_en: Then I asked new agents to compare the completed implementations and look for interesting deviations. It was shocking how many important decisions the agents never asked about but simply made—and made differently.

sentences.en: Then I asked new agents to compare the completed implementations and look for interesting deviations.
sentences.zh: 然后我要求新代理比较已完成的实现并寻找有趣的偏差。

sentences.en: It was shocking how many important decisions the agents never asked about but simply made—and made differently.
sentences.zh: 令人震惊的是，有那么多重要的决定，特工们从未询问过，只是简单地做出了——而且做出了不同的决定。

blocks.html_en: Here’s an example. Replication uses the fairly obvious approach: Catch up by asking for everything since the last known entry, and then long poll for new entries. There’s one ugly twist: database rollbacks. Rare, but they do happen, and they break the “append-only” contract.

sentences.en: Here’s an example.
sentences.zh: 这是一个例子。

sentences.en: Replication uses the fairly obvious approach: Catch up by asking for everything since the last known entry, and then long poll for new entries.
sentences.zh: 复制使用相当明显的方法：通过询问自上次已知条目以来的所有内容来赶上，然后长时间轮询新条目。

sentences.en: There’s one ugly twist: database rollbacks.
sentences.zh: 有一个丑陋的转折：数据库回滚。

sentences.en: Rare, but they do happen, and they break the “append-only” contract.
sentences.zh: 很少见，但它们确实发生了，并且它们打破了“仅附加”合同。

blocks.html_en: The agents noticed this, and they solved it in wildly different ways. The design I ultimately settled on was to give every row a “timeline” field, as in “which timeline are you living in?” These are randomly generated, and every sync request for “entries since row N” includes the edge server’s timeline value for row N. If there’s a timeline mismatch, we know that history has been altered and fall back to a full clean re-sync.

sentences.en: The agents noticed this, and they solved it in wildly different ways.
sentences.zh: 特工们注意到了这一点，并以截然不同的方式解决了这个问题。

sentences.en: The design I ultimately settled on was to give every row a “timeline” field, as in “which timeline are you living in?”
sentences.zh: 我最终确定的设计是为每一行提供一个“时间线”字段，例如“您生活在哪个时间线？”

sentences.en: These are randomly generated, and every sync request for “entries since row N” includes the edge server’s timeline value for row N.
sentences.zh: 这些是随机生成的，并且“自第 N 行以来的条目”的每个同步请求都包含边缘服务器的第 N 行时间线值。

sentences.en: If there’s a timeline mismatch, we know that history has been altered and fall back to a full clean re-sync.
sentences.zh: 如果存在时间线不匹配，我们知道历史已被更改并回退到完全干净的重新同步。

blocks.html_en: There were also obvious style differences between the systems built by different agents. Claude and Codex both agreed that Claude created a more elegant system but that Codex was more thorough.

sentences.en: There were also obvious style differences between the systems built by different agents.
sentences.zh: 不同代理构建的系统之间也存在明显的风格差异。

sentences.en: Claude and Codex both agreed that Claude created a more elegant system but that Codex was more thorough.
sentences.zh: 克劳德和 Codex 都同意克劳德创建了一个更优雅的系统，但 Codex 更彻底。

blocks.html_en: I worked through the list of major identified divergences, experimented, and then added more written guidance.

sentences.en: I worked through the list of major identified divergences, experimented, and then added more written guidance.
sentences.zh: 我仔细研究了已发现的主要分歧列表，进行了实验，然后添加了更多书面指导。

blocks.html_en: Then I repeated that entire differential spec analysis process, twice. I know my aphorisms.

sentences.en: Then I repeated that entire differential spec analysis process, twice.
sentences.zh: 然后我重复了整个差分规格分析过程两次。

sentences.en: I know my aphorisms.
sentences.zh: 我知道我的格言。

blocks.html_en:
Plan to throw one away; you will, anyhow.

— Fred Brooks

If you plan to throw one away, you will throw away two.

— Craig Zerouni

sentences.en: Plan to throw one away; you will, anyhow. — Fred Brooks If you plan to throw one away, you will throw away two. — Craig Zerouni
sentences.zh: 计划扔掉一个；无论如何，你会的。 — Fred Brooks 如果你打算扔掉一个，你就会扔掉两个。 — 克雷格·泽罗尼

blocks.html_en: Plan to throw one away; you will, anyhow.

sentences.en: Plan to throw one away; you will, anyhow.
sentences.zh: 计划扔掉一个；无论如何，你会的。

blocks.html_en: — Fred Brooks

sentences.en: — Fred Brooks
sentences.zh: — 弗雷德·布鲁克斯

blocks.html_en: If you plan to throw one away, you will throw away two.

sentences.en: If you plan to throw one away, you will throw away two.
sentences.zh: 如果你打算扔掉一个，你就会扔掉两个。

blocks.html_en: — Craig Zerouni

sentences.en: — Craig Zerouni
sentences.zh: — 克雷格·泽罗尼

blocks.html_en: By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.

sentences.en: By the time I was ready to build a keeper, I had accumulated a scar-tissue document that was empirically sufficient to guide an agent through most of the important decisions, at every layer, ranging from high level goals through architecture down to the occasional low level detail, such as the exact shape of the data type for load-bearing concurrent caches.
sentences.zh: 当我准备好构建一个 keeper 时，我已经积累了一份伤疤组织文档，该文档在经验上足以指导代理完成每一层的大多数重要决策，范围从高层目标到架构，再到偶尔的低层细节，例如承载并发缓存的数据类型的确切形状。

blocks.html_en: The final system included unit tests, end-to-end tests, a shadow-mode for de-risking prod rollout, and a terse written-by-and-for-agents doc suite.

sentences.en: The final system included unit tests, end-to-end tests, a shadow-mode for de-risking prod rollout, and a terse written-by-and-for-agents doc suite.
sentences.zh: 最终的系统包括单元测试、端到端测试、用于降低产品推出风险的影子模式，以及由代理编写并为代理编写的简洁文档套件。

blocks.html_en: This cumulatively took about a week of my attention. I read a vanishingly small amount of the actual code.

sentences.en: This cumulatively took about a week of my attention.
sentences.zh: 这总共花费了我大约一周的注意力。

sentences.en: I read a vanishingly small amount of the actual code.
sentences.zh: 我读了极少量的实际代码。

blocks.html_en: At the end of that, I presented the solution to the team. I planned to launch the server and then go on vacation. As my colleagues peppered me with questions—"How does X work? What happens in condition Y?"—I found I could answer all of them confidently. (And I did go on that vacation. Number of DNS incidents a month later: 0.)

sentences.en: At the end of that, I presented the solution to the team.
sentences.zh: 最后，我向团队提出了解决方案。

sentences.en: I planned to launch the server and then go on vacation.
sentences.zh: 我打算启动服务器然后去度假。

sentences.en: As my colleagues peppered me with questions—"How does X work?
sentences.zh: 当我的同事们向我提出一系列问题时——“X 是如何工作的？

sentences.en: What happens in condition Y?"—I found I could answer all of them confidently.
sentences.zh: 在 Y 条件下会发生什么？”——我发现我可以自信地回答所有这些问题。

sentences.en: (And I did go on that vacation.
sentences.zh: （我确实去度假了。

sentences.en: Number of DNS incidents a month later: 0.)
sentences.zh: 一个月后 DNS 事件数：0。）

blocks.html_en: Claude wasn’t just a compiler here. I never handed off a task and let an agent make a bunch of decisions in order to reduce it to practice. That’s vibe-coding.

sentences.en: Claude wasn’t just a compiler here.
sentences.zh: 克劳德不仅仅是一个编译器。

sentences.en: I never handed off a task and let an agent make a bunch of decisions in order to reduce it to practice.
sentences.zh: 我从来没有把任务交给代理人，让代理人做出一堆决定，以便将其付诸实践。

sentences.en: That’s vibe-coding.
sentences.zh: 这就是振动编码。

blocks.html_en: Rather, Claude was a vertically integrated resource, a multi-compiler. Its ability to work across the stack accelerated and augmented my ability to make a bunch of decisions at different levels, including about which decisions were important. (Most individual lines of code don’t make that cut.) That’s vibe-engineering.

sentences.en: Rather, Claude was a vertically integrated resource, a multi-compiler.
sentences.zh: 相反，克劳德是一个垂直整合的资源，一个多编译器。

sentences.en: Its ability to work across the stack accelerated and augmented my ability to make a bunch of decisions at different levels, including about which decisions were important.
sentences.zh: 它跨堆栈工作的能力加速并增强了我在不同级别做出一系列决策的能力，包括哪些决策是重要的。

sentences.en: (Most individual lines of code don’t make that cut.)
sentences.zh: （大多数单独的代码行都无法做到这一点。）

sentences.en: That’s vibe-engineering.
sentences.zh: 这就是氛围工程。

blocks.html_en: I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code. Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve. But I won't have to. And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work. And there’s an enduring artifact that encapsulates the central, intentional aspects of the design that were important enough to record, across all layers, and should thus survive bug fixes and code churn.

sentences.en: I’d say that, in all the ways that matter, I understand the code.
sentences.zh: 我想说的是，在所有重要的方面，我理解代码。

sentences.en: Sure, if I had to hand-edit it now, there’d be a serious learning curve.
sentences.zh: 当然，如果我现在必须手动编辑它，就会有一个严重的学习曲线。

sentences.en: But I won't have to.
sentences.zh: 但我不必这么做。

sentences.en: And more importantly, I can reason about the system, share perspectives with my colleagues, and guide agents on future work.
sentences.zh: 更重要的是，我可以对系统进行推理，与同事分享观点，并指导座席未来的工作。

sentences.en: And there’s an enduring artifact that encapsulates the central, intentional aspects of the design that were important enough to record, across all layers, and should thus survive bug fixes and code churn.
sentences.zh: 并且有一个持久的工件封装了设计的核心、有意的方面，这些方面对于跨所有层进行记录非常重要，因此应该能够在错误修复和代码改动中幸存下来。

blocks.html_en: One of the questions of this era is: What do software engineers need to understand about the systems they work on?

sentences.en: One of the questions of this era is: What do software engineers need to understand about the systems they work on?
sentences.zh: 这个时代的问题之一是：软件工程师需要了解他们所工作的系统的哪些内容？

blocks.html_en: Well-chosen layers provide understanding. Fundamental laws of physics appear all-encompassing, but they’re inferior to classical mechanics for explaining why it’s better to be in a bus than a car in an accident.

sentences.en: Well-chosen layers provide understanding.
sentences.zh: 精心选择的层可以提供理解。

sentences.en: Fundamental laws of physics appear all-encompassing, but they’re inferior to classical mechanics for explaining why it’s better to be in a bus than a car in an accident.
sentences.zh: 基本物理定律似乎包罗万象，但在解释为什么发生事故时坐在公共汽车上比坐在汽车里更好时，它们不如经典力学。

blocks.html_en: Some software layers are dying, because they provide convenience, but not extra insight. (Sorry, Tailwind. I loved you.) But software layers that enable us to express important decisions in a comprehensible way? Those will stay.

sentences.en: Some software layers are dying, because they provide convenience, but not extra insight.
sentences.zh: 一些软件层正在消亡，因为它们提供了便利，但没有提供额外的洞察力。

sentences.en: (Sorry, Tailwind.
sentences.zh: （对不起，顺风。

sentences.en: I loved you.)
sentences.zh: 我爱你。）

sentences.en: But software layers that enable us to express important decisions in a comprehensible way?
sentences.zh: 但是软件层能让我们以易于理解的方式表达重要决策吗？

sentences.en: Those will stay.
sentences.zh: 那些会留下来。

blocks.html_en: We are shifting more of our attention up the stack, but without fully relinquishing the lower layers. Agents are not a free pass to hand off all understanding of the deeper layers of a system. Most of the Go standard library is written in Go, but a few key routines are written in assembly. You can’t rely on the compiler there.

sentences.en: We are shifting more of our attention up the stack, but without fully relinquishing the lower layers.
sentences.zh: 我们正在将更多的注意力转移到堆栈上，但没有完全放弃较低的层。

sentences.en: Agents are not a free pass to hand off all understanding of the deeper layers of a system.
sentences.zh: 代理并不是传递对系统更深层次的所有理解的免费通行证。

sentences.en: Most of the Go standard library is written in Go, but a few key routines are written in assembly.
sentences.zh: 大多数 Go 标准库都是用 Go 编写的，但一些关键例程是用汇编语言编写的。

sentences.en: You can’t rely on the compiler there.
sentences.zh: 你不能依赖那里的编译器。

blocks.html_en: Software engineers are being stretched. It’s exhilarating and exhausting. What’s becoming clear, though, is that in the near future, vibe-engineering is just…engineering.

sentences.en: Software engineers are being stretched.
sentences.zh: 软件工程师正面临着压力。

sentences.en: It’s exhilarating and exhausting.
sentences.zh: 这是令人兴奋和疲惫的。

sentences.en: What’s becoming clear, though, is that in the near future, vibe-engineering is just…engineering.
sentences.zh: 不过，越来越清楚的是，在不久的将来，振动工程只是……工程。

content_html_zh:
2025 年初，我写了《克劳德是编译器吗？》 当时我的回答是：我不知道。

我现在很确定答案是“不，这是一个类别错误，它比编译器更好。” 但这需要一些拆包。

计算机程序是出了名的复杂和挑剔。 程序以极高的精度运行。 没有“挥手”CPU 指令。 与此同时，高层目标却非常不明确。

在高度程式化的世界观中，软件是分层构建的，每一层都添加规范并隐藏“不必要的”细节。 愿景变成战略，产品计划变成编码计划，代码变成二进制文件。 每个步骤都由不同的角色处理：执行人员、副总裁、项目经理、架构师、工程师、编译器。

至关重要的是，每一步都涉及做出很多决定。 这就是提高规范水平的意义。 （这就是为什么我招聘工程师的两个关键指标之一是判断力。 另一个是礼让。）

从源代码到二进制的底层是编译器的工作。 编译器做出很多决定！ 内联、寄存器分配、是否发出警告或彻底拒绝程序。 这些决策很重要：它们推动性能、系统稳定性、可预测性和故障模式。 编译器工程师的工作是安排编译器做出一致的正确决策。

一个好的、值得信赖的编译器可以让软件工程师不必做出这些决定。 大多数工程师对编译器的工作原理知之甚少；他们不需要这样做就能有效。

2025 年，我们使用 LLM 生成较小的代码块。 在这种心理模型中，编码代理可能会作为软件工程师和传统编译器之间的新层插入。 它将自然语言“编译”为代码，从而使工程师无需做出决策。 它的价值与其可靠性和它可以做出的决策的规模成正比。

问题是，这种高度程式化的世界观是错误的。 抽象泄漏和层次摩擦。 即使他们不这样做，我们也会在他们身上戳洞。

跨层工作非常有价值；机械同情很重要。

帝国大厦的部分建造过程在不到一年的时间内且低于预算（！！） 是通过系统地跨层工作。 例如，在决定外部铬镍钢包层时：

在没有充分协商的情况下，建筑师、建造商和分包商都认为自己没有能力处理这一复杂的施工技术问题。 因此，经过充分的初步讨论后，召开了一次全体会议，参加会议的有业主代表、建筑师和建造商、轧制材料的分包商、制造和安装金属工人以及在准备的几个阶段对所有板材进行测试的检查员。

当你大声说出来时，这听起来真的很明显。

然而，我们在实践中却系统性地失败了。 我只能想象那些金属工人有机会引导设计走向不那么缓慢和痛苦的工作的喜悦。

我们失败的部分原因是不知道什么是值得问的。 最优秀的高管对其行业有着深入的了解，这是有原因的。 我还怀疑其中一些是不屑一顾（“生产线金属工人能告诉我什么？”）。 但很大一部分还在于沟通和组织开销。 层的存在是有原因的——信息隐藏可以实现组织的扩展。

Claude 比编译器更好，因为它可以跨堆栈垂直工作。 法学硕士现在讨论战略、产品、架构、代码和机器代码。 还不能（还？） 它可以像经验丰富、专注的人一样完成大多数个人任务，但它可以完成所有这些任务，而无需安排会议或征求许可。

这是一个具体的例子。

exe.dev VM 有一个很好听的域名：vm-name.exe.xyz。 当我们启动新的虚拟机时，我们会添加一个或三个 CNAME 条目。 容易，对吧？

但我们的虚拟机启动速度非常快，即使我们在创建虚拟机之前创建了 DNS 条目，我们的用户仍然必须等待 DNS 传播，这有时需要几分钟而不是几秒钟。

我们做了一件显而易见的事情：我们编写了自己的 DNS 服务器，以便 DNS 始终立即匹配事实来源。 生活是美好的。

但延迟很重要，因此我们添加了区域。 就这样，DNS 再次成为长杆，因为所有 DNS 均由俄勒冈州提供服务。 此外，部署还导致了轻微的 DNS 中断。 为了解决这个问题，我们现在需要的是一个地理上分散但完全一致的 DNS 服务器。

我们做了一个明智的工程师在面对难题时会做的事情：作弊。 我们根据我们的特定需求设计了一个分布式 DNS 服务器。

目标很明确：减少远离俄勒冈州的用户的延迟并提高正常运行时间的弹性。 但其余的则不然。 我们必须弄清楚一切，从我们想要的确切行为（特别是在各种故障条件下），到它如何适应我们的整体公司计划，到最能实现这些目标的架构，一直到精细的实施细节。

我们亲自讨论了最高级别的战略和架构决策。 我们将制作一个相当通用的 DNS 服务器，并根据我们的特定行为调整进行分层，使用中心辐射模型，使用仅附加复制策略，并在边缘具有持久性。

剩下的就是实际构建它。

我让法学硕士研究分布式 DNS 系统的标准设计，教我 DNS 的本质和怪癖，指出历史上的安全缺陷，探索替代实施策略（AXFR/IXFR？ 不，谢谢），研究开源产品、游戏失败模式并计划测试策略。

一旦我有了一个看起来有希望的设计的初步草图，我就提示多个并发代理循环来构建整个东西，包括测试和对抗性代码审查。 他们提出了一系列问题——从主要结构方法到行级代码问题，涉及各个细节级别。 当我回答这些问题（或者恢复那些引起遗憾的答案）时，我慢慢地将我所学到的知识转化为非常简洁的书面指导，将事实证明很重要的决定编入法典。

然后我要求新代理比较已完成的实现并寻找有趣的偏差。 令人震惊的是，有那么多重要的决定，特工们从未询问过，只是简单地做出了——而且做出了不同的决定。

这是一个例子。 复制使用相当明显的方法：通过询问自上次已知条目以来的所有内容来赶上，然后长时间轮询新条目。 有一个丑陋的转折：数据库回滚。 很少见，但它们确实发生了，并且它们打破了“仅附加”合同。

特工们注意到了这一点，并以截然不同的方式解决了这个问题。 我最终确定的设计是为每一行提供一个“时间线”字段，例如“您生活在哪个时间线？” 这些是随机生成的，并且“自第 N 行以来的条目”的每个同步请求都包含边缘服务器的第 N 行时间线值。 如果存在时间线不匹配，我们知道历史已被更改并回退到完全干净的重新同步。

不同代理构建的系统之间也存在明显的风格差异。 克劳德和 Codex 都同意克劳德创建了一个更优雅的系统，但 Codex 更彻底。

我仔细研究了已发现的主要分歧列表，进行了实验，然后添加了更多书面指导。

然后我重复了整个差分规格分析过程两次。 我知道我的格言。

计划扔掉一个；无论如何，你会的。 — Fred Brooks 如果你打算扔掉一个，你就会扔掉两个。 — 克雷格·泽罗尼

当我准备好构建一个 keeper 时，我已经积累了一份伤疤组织文档，该文档在经验上足以指导代理完成每一层的大多数重要决策，范围从高层目标到架构，再到偶尔的低层细节，例如承载并发缓存的数据类型的确切形状。

最终的系统包括单元测试、端到端测试、用于降低产品推出风险的影子模式，以及由代理编写并为代理编写的简洁文档套件。

这总共花费了我大约一周的注意力。 我读了极少量的实际代码。

最后，我向团队提出了解决方案。 我打算启动服务器然后去度假。 当我的同事们向我提出一系列问题时——“X 是如何工作的？ 在 Y 条件下会发生什么？”——我发现我可以自信地回答所有这些问题。 （我确实去度假了。 一个月后 DNS 事件数：0。）

克劳德不仅仅是一个编译器。 我从来没有把任务交给代理人，让代理人做出一堆决定，以便将其付诸实践。 这就是振动编码。

相反，克劳德是一个垂直整合的资源，一个多编译器。 它跨堆栈工作的能力加速并增强了我在不同级别做出一系列决策的能力，包括哪些决策是重要的。 （大多数单独的代码行都无法做到这一点。） 这就是氛围工程。

我想说的是，在所有重要的方面，我理解代码。 当然，如果我现在必须手动编辑它，就会有一个严重的学习曲线。 但我不必这么做。 更重要的是，我可以对系统进行推理，与同事分享观点，并指导座席未来的工作。 并且有一个持久的工件封装了设计的核心、有意的方面，这些方面对于跨所有层进行记录非常重要，因此应该能够在错误修复和代码改动中幸存下来。

这个时代的问题之一是：软件工程师需要了解他们所工作的系统的哪些内容？

精心选择的层可以提供理解。 基本物理定律似乎包罗万象，但在解释为什么发生事故时坐在公共汽车上比坐在汽车里更好时，它们不如经典力学。

一些软件层正在消亡，因为它们提供了便利，但没有提供额外的洞察力。 （对不起，顺风。 我爱你。） 但是软件层能让我们以易于理解的方式表达重要决策吗？ 那些会留下来。

我们正在将更多的注意力转移到堆栈上，但没有完全放弃较低的层。 代理并不是传递对系统更深层次的所有理解的免费通行证。 大多数 Go 标准库都是用 Go 编写的，但一些关键例程是用汇编语言编写的。 你不能依赖那里的编译器。

软件工程师正面临着压力。 这是令人兴奋和疲惫的。 不过，越来越清楚的是，在不久的将来，振动工程只是……工程。
